---
title: "mu over g madness"
author: "Andy"
date: "2025-10-06"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r}
# If both curves are linear, there will be no intermediate solutions for mu/g OR b - d
# Hold growth slope constant, and intercepts constant
growth_slope <- -0.15
growth_int <- 1
mort_int <- 0.4
trait_vec <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
growth_line <- growth_slope * trait_vec + growth_int
mort_slope <- seq(-0.4, 0, by = 0.005)

mu_g.df <- as.data.frame(mat.or.vec( length(mort_slope), 3 ) )
colnames(mu_g.df) <- c("mort_slope", "bd_optim", "mu_g_optim")

for (i in 1:length( mort_slope ) ){
  mu_g.df[ i, 1 ] <- mort_slope[i]
  
  mort_line <- mort_slope[i] * trait_vec + mort_int
  bd_optim_val <- growth_line - mort_line
  mu_g_optim_val <- mort_line / growth_line
  mu_g.df[ i, 2 ] <- trait_vec[ which.max( bd_optim_val ) ]
  mu_g.df[ i, 3 ] <- trait_vec[ which.min( mu_g_optim_val ) ]
}

# They work out exactly the same but the inverse of one another
plot( x = mort_slope, y = mu_g.df$bd_optim) 
# Exhibit full response until mortality slope is smaller (more negative) than growth slope 
# I.e., reduction in mortality > reduction in growth
plot( x = mort_slope, y = mu_g.df$mu_g_optim) 
# Exhibit full response until mu/g at zero trait expression exceeds mu/g at full trait expression
# I.e., qualitatively similar but the switchpoint is in a different place
```
